config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically come before the rights of the individual, because once you give society too much power, it can easily be used to silence minorities and justify abuse. In my view, a healthy society protects individual rights first, then limits them only when there is a clear and necessary public reason. From my perspective, community matters a lot, but it should not become an excuse to crush personal freedom.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The rights of the individual should not be sacrificed just because society wants a cleaner or easier answer. From my perspective in the United States, our history makes it pretty clear that appeals to "the common good" have often been used to excuse censorship, discrimination, and abuse of power, especially against minorities. Society absolutely has legitimate interests in public safety, justice, and shared rules, but those interests should justify limits on individual rights only when the case is narrow, necessary, and accountable. If society always comes first, then individual liberty becomes conditional on whatever the majority feels like doing that day, and I do not trust that.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual, because in Indonesia I have seen how easily “public interest” can be used to pressure minorities and silence inconvenient voices. At the same time, I do not think individual rights are absolute in every case, because real social life also needs order, safety, and mutual responsibility. The right balance is to protect individual rights as the default, and only limit them when there is a clear, necessary, and fair public reason. If society comes first too often, it becomes too easy for the majority to bully everyone else in the name of harmony.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take automatic priority over the rights of the individual, because in the United States I have seen how easily “the public good” can become a blanket excuse for overreach. That said, I do not believe individual rights are absolute in every situation: laws against violence, fraud, and genuine threats to public safety are legitimate because they protect everyone’s basic freedom. What I reject is the idea that society gets a default trump card, since that usually means the majority can steamroll minorities whenever it is convenient. To me, the right standard is that individual rights come first unless there is a narrow, necessary, and clearly justified reason to limit them.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority by default, because in Indonesia “social harmony” is often used to pressure people into silence instead of solving the real problem. I do accept that individual rights are not absolute, since public safety, law, and basic order do matter, but limits on rights need to be narrow, fair, and easy to challenge. If society always comes first, the majority can hide behind community language while minorities pay the price. For me, the safer principle is that individual rights come first, and society must justify any exception very carefully.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual, because in the United States I have seen how easily that idea turns into majority rule with a nicer name. Public safety and basic order are real needs, but they do not justify giving society a blank check to silence dissent, target minorities, or excuse government overreach. I do not buy the argument that “social harmony” or “the common good” should come first by default, because that usually means the people with the least power pay the highest price. The better rule is that individual rights are the starting point, and any limit on them has to be narrow, necessary, and tightly accountable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
